perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall procure that its employees, agents and sub -contractors to whom Proprietary Information is disclosed or who have access to Proprietary Information sign a nondisclosure or similar ag reement in content substantially similar to this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall encourage its employees, agents, and sub-contractors to whom Proprietary Information is disclosed or who have access to Proprietary Information to consider signing a nondisclosure or similar agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'shall procure' (implying a mandatory obligation to ensure signing) to 'shall encourage' (implying a suggestion). It also removes the 'substantially similar' requirement, making the obligation weaker and more ambiguous. The phrase 'to consider signing' adds more ambiguity and makes it non-compulsory to sign a nondisclosure or similar agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act aims to protect trade secrets. By weakening the requirement for the Receiving Party to ensure its employees, agents, and subcontractors are bound by nondisclosure agreements, the modified text reduces the protection of Proprietary Information, making it harder to enforce the protection of trade secrets under the EEA if a breach occurs.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the Receiving Party to 'procure' that its employees, agents, and subcontractors sign a nondisclosure agreement, implying a compulsory obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'encourage' signing, and adds 'to consider signing,' introducing ambiguity and making it non-compulsory. The Law Explanation states that weakening the requirement for nondisclosure agreements reduces the protection of Proprietary Information, potentially undermining the Economic Espionage Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Economic Espionage Act of 1996. Because the change significantly reduces the mandatory nature of ensuring nondisclosure agreements, it directly contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of eighteen months following the execution of this agreement to the Receiving Party shall not, directly or indirectly hire, solicit, or encourage to leave the Disclosing Party employm ent, any employee, contractor or player of the Disclosing Party or hire any such employee or contractor who has left the Disclosing Parties employment, contractual engagement or player within one year of such employment or engagement , the Receiving Party .
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of eighteen months following the execution of this agreement, the Receiving Party should generally avoid hiring, soliciting, or encouraging to leave the Disclosing Party's employment any employee, contractor, or player of the Disclosing Party, or hire any such employee or contractor who has left the Disclosing Parties employment, contractual engagement, or player within one year of such employment or engagement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'should generally avoid' weakens the restriction, making it non-binding. The addition of 'generally' makes the clause ambiguous and unenforceable. This weakens the non-solicitation agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions (like the sale of a business), a broad non-solicitation agreement is often viewed skeptically. By weakening the clause to 'should generally avoid', it introduces uncertainty and makes it more difficult to enforce the already potentially questionable restraint of trade, contradicting the spirit of Section 16600 that favors open competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'should generally avoid' significantly weakens the non-solicitation agreement. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a) states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' with limited exceptions. By making the non-solicitation clause ambiguous and less binding, the modified text undermines the original intent to restrain solicitation, increasing the likelihood of violating Section 16600. The original text, with 'shall not,' created a clear restraint, whereas the changed text is more lenient, contradicting the statute's disapproval of overly broad restraints on trade, especially as interpreted in Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP, as noted in 16600(b)(1).

perturbation.original_text: For a period of three years following the execution of this agreement , the contractor shall not start up his/her own cricket related business using the customer lists, trade secrets, inventions, innovations, processes, information, records an d specificatio ns of the Disclosing Party and/or used by the Disclosing party in connection with the operation of its business including, without limitation, the Disclosing parties business and product processes, methods, accounts and procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of three years following the execution of this agreement, the contractor will endeavor not to start up their own cricket related business using, to a significant extent, the customer lists, trade secrets, inventions, innovations, processes, information, records, and specifications of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'shall not' is changed to 'will endeavor not to', weakening the obligation to use best efforts, not a prohibition. Adding 'to a significant extent' introduces ambiguity about how much usage is permissible, making the restriction vague and harder to enforce. The removal of "and/or used by the Disclosing party in connection with the operation of its business including, without limitation, the Disclosing parties business and product processes, methods, accounts and procedures." removes important clarifications.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines and protects trade secrets. The phrase 'to a significant extent' introduces ambiguity that makes it difficult to determine what constitutes misappropriation of trade secrets, thus weakening the protection afforded by the UTSA. The alteration undermines the clarity required to establish a violation of trade secret law.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'will endeavor not to' significantly weakens the obligation. Adding 'to a significant extent' introduces ambiguity about the permissible level of usage, undermining the original restriction's clarity and enforceability. The removal of the clarification phrase "and/or used by the Disclosing party in connection with the operation of its business including, without limitation, the Disclosing parties business and product processes, methods, accounts and procedures." further diminishes the scope of the restriction. The scraped law snippets are not directly relevant in this case, however, given the Perturbation and Law Explanation focus on weakening and creating ambiguity regarding the protection of trade secrets, a contradiction exists as the changes make it difficult to determine a violation.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may make disclosure s required by law or court order provided the Receiving Party uses diligent reasonable efforts to limit disclosure and has allowed the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may make disclosures required by law or court order, provided the Receiving Party considers the possibility of limiting disclosure and attempts to allow the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'diligent reasonable efforts' to limit disclosure, a relatively high standard. The modified text weakens this to 'considers the possibility of limiting disclosure', which is subjective and easily satisfied. Similarly, 'has allowed' implies a positive action to facilitate a protective order, while 'attempts to allow' implies a weaker, less committed effort. The word 'attempts' is ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c) governs protective orders. While it doesn't explicitly mandate a party seek a protective order, it anticipates that parties will actively seek to protect confidential information from disclosure. By weakening the obligation to merely 'attempts to allow', the modified text may not satisfy a court's expectation of reasonable efforts to protect confidential information, especially when the disclosing party could have taken concrete steps.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 'diligent reasonable efforts' to limit disclosure, and that the Receiving Party 'has allowed' the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order. The modified version uses the phrases 'considers the possibility of limiting disclosure' and 'attempts to allow', which are weaker and more ambiguous, and thus create a legal contradiction by weakening the original obligation, and undermining the expectation that parties actively protect confidential information. The LLM explanation stated that 'By weakening the obligation to merely 'attempts to allow', the modified text may not satisfy a court's expectation of reasonable efforts to protect confidential information, especially when the disclosing party could have taken concrete steps.'

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be gover ned by the laws of the jurisdiction in which the Disclosing Party is located (or if the Disclosing Party is based in more than one country, the country in which its headquarters are located) (the "Territory") and the parties agree to submit disputes arisin g out of or in connection with this Agreement to the non -exclusive of the courts in the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally interpreted in accordance with legal principles that the parties believe are commonly held internationally. The parties may consider submitting disputes arising out of this agreement to any mutually agreeable dispute resolution process.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to 'legal principles that the parties believe are commonly held internationally' is extremely vague and unenforceable. The original specifies a clear jurisdiction; the modified version has no clear legal basis. Substituting 'non-exclusive of the courts in the Territory' with 'may consider submitting disputes...to any mutually agreeable dispute resolution process' further diminishes the enforceable nature of dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) favors enforcing arbitration agreements. However, an agreement to 'consider' arbitration is not an agreement to arbitrate. The modified text's vague language does not meet the FAA's requirement for a clear and unequivocal agreement to arbitrate, undermining the enforceability of any dispute resolution process. Furthermore, the law mandates that the parties adhere to the contract, something that is not guaranteed with the changed text.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a clear jurisdiction for disputes, making it enforceable. The modified text replaces this with a vague reference to 'legal principles that the parties believe are commonly held internationally' and an agreement to 'consider' dispute resolution. The FAA, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires a 'written provision' to 'settle by arbitration,' indicating a clear agreement to arbitrate. The modified text's language of 'consider' is not a clear agreement, undermining the enforceability of dispute resolution, creating a contradiction. The changed text diminishes the legally enforceable nature of dispute resolution.
